feedSummary.title: Live Policy Feed
feedSummary.body: The current policy watchlist is still thin but usable: 5 explicit agriculture or trade-adjacent items are live, led by USDA's May 15 modified-organisms request for information and Commerce's May 14 BASF foreign-trade-zone filing. That is enough to keep policy on the dashboard, but not enough to override the current energy-led market posture.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Policy Feed Relevance and Procurement Transmission
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Rank whether the current policy feed is actually capable of changing soybean-oil buyer behavior or is still just a monitoring surface.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Policy items only deserve posture-changing weight when they are both procurement-relevant and dense enough to imply persistence rather than administrative noise.
quantResearchProtocol: Build a relevance-screened current item set, not a raw feed dump.
Count how many items are truly agriculture/trade adjacent.
Separate administrative notices from transmission-relevant items.
Compare the resulting policy pressure with the live market lead channel.
inferenceConstraints: Do not treat raw item count as equal to procurement pressure.
Do not hide when policy is secondary to market-driven cost pressure.
outputRequirements: State the count of relevant live items.
Identify the lead current item and why it matters.
Explain whether policy is lead-driver or secondary-driver status.

sourceRecords.recordHint: Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026

notes: Only procurement-relevant agriculture and trade items were kept in the snapshot.
Policy remains secondary to energy in the current buyer posture.

sourcePressure.title: Source Activity
sourcePressure.body: Source breadth is concentrated rather than broad: Agriculture Department contributes 3 of the 5 current items, or 60.0% of the visible feed, while Commerce and Congress each contribute one. That concentration keeps confidence moderate at best; relevance is there, but corroboration breadth is not.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Measure whether current source concentration supports a confident policy read or demands caution.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A thin policy tape can still be useful, but high source concentration lowers confidence unless multiple institutions confirm the same transmission path.
quantResearchProtocol: Count current visible items by source.
Compute source-share concentration for the lead source.
Classify the window as concentrated or broad.
Use that classification to adjust policy confidence.
inferenceConstraints: Do not overstate confidence from a concentrated source mix.
Do not confuse concentration with irrelevance.
outputRequirements: Report source count and lead-source share.
Explain the buyer consequence of concentrated sourcing.

sourceRecords.recordHint: three USDA-led current items in visible policy set
one congressional anchor item in visible policy set

notes: Source concentration math is explicit inside the card.
Confidence is tied to breadth, not just raw item relevance.

tagPressure.title: Policy Tag Pressure
tagPressure.body: The current theme map is led by policy tags on all 5 items, with trade-adjacent coding on 2 of 5 and agriculture context on 2 of 5. That says the feed is more administrative than shock-driven; buyers should watch trade mechanics and USDA process, not expect an immediate legislative cost wave.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Identify which policy themes deserve the most buyer attention in the current window.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When the live policy taxonomy is dominated by process and trade tags rather than explicit soybean-oil mandates, buyers should prioritize monitoring over posture changes.
quantResearchProtocol: Count tag frequency across the current relevance-screened item set.
Separate process-policy, trade-policy, and agriculture-context tags.
Classify the current taxonomy as shock-oriented or administrative.
Translate the outcome into a buyer watch priority list.
inferenceConstraints: Do not pretend the tag set is richer than it is.
Do not infer a soybean-oil policy shock without explicit theme support.
Do not use generic policy wording without naming the active tags.
outputRequirements: State the leading tags and counts.
Explain whether the taxonomy is administrative or shock-oriented.
Provide the buyer watch priority that follows.

sourceRecords.recordHint: USDA and Commerce items used for trade/policy tag counts
farm bill anchor item used for macro/policy tag count

notes: Theme ranking is based on the actual visible current item set.
The current taxonomy is informative but not dense enough for regime-calling.
